coredata.dc:description: An optical fiber acetone gas sensor based on N-CQDs@In2O3 porous ordered thin film is proposed. The probe adopts the hollow malposition structure (HMS) to excite the evanescent field on the surface of hollow-core fiber (HCF). N-CQDs@In2O3 porous and ordered thin film was formed on the surface of optical fiber after calcining polystyrene (PS) microspheres at high temperature. The experimental results reveal the ultraviolet (UV) sensitization function for the proposed sensor at room temperature. The enhanced sensitivity is 7.1 pm/ppm, which is ∼5.9 times of the spiritual density of unsensitized sensor. In addition, the sensor has a limit of detection (LOD) of 500 ppb and excellent time response characteristics (response time 6.2 s and recovery time 7.1 s). The sensitization mechanism of N-CQDs to In2O3 is explained by the results of the universal density function (DFT). The frontier electron orbital studies show that N-CQDs@In2O3 has more abundant electron clouds. The good moisture resistance enables its stable performance in humid environment. UV sensitization is an effective way to improve the sensitivity of optical fiber gas sensor, which is of great significance for detecting the acetone gas with ultra-low concentration.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An optical fiber acetone gas sensor based on N-CQDs@In2O3 porous ordered thin film is proposed. The probe adopts the hollow malposition structure (HMS) to excite the evanescent field on the surface of hollow-core fiber (HCF). N-CQDs@In2O3 porous and ordered thin film was formed on the surface of optical fiber after calcining polystyrene (PS) microspheres at high temperature. The experimental results reveal the ultraviolet (UV) sensitization function for the proposed sensor at room temperature. The enhanced sensitivity is 7.1 pm/ppm, which is ∼5.9 times of the spiritual density of unsensitized sensor. In addition, the sensor has a limit of detection (LOD) of 500 ppb and excellent time response characteristics (response time 6.2 s and recovery time 7.1 s). The sensitization mechanism of N-CQDs to In2O3 is explained by the results of the universal density function (DFT). The frontier electron orbital studies show that N-CQDs@In2O3 has more abundant electron clouds. The good moisture resistance enables its stable performance in humid environment. UV sensitization is an effective way to improve the sensitivity of optical fiber gas sensor, which is of great significance for detecting the acetone gas with ultra-low concentration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetone is a widely used volatile organic compound [1], which is often used as a solvent in industrial production and is used to prepare dyes [2], paints [3], cosmetics [4] and so on. The volatilization threshold of acetone is 450 mg/cm3 (569000 ppm) [5], and it is easy to enter human body through respiratory tract, causing dizziness [6], nerve paralysis [7] and other discomfort symptoms. At the same time, short-term high-concentration exposure to acetone will irritate skin and eyes [8], while long-term exposure can cause organ failure [9]. In addition, acetone is flammable and can cause explosion at a certain concentration (2.6 %) [10], which brings potential safety hazards. Therefore, acetone has a negative impact on human health and safety, and how to achieve efficient detection of acetone gas is particularly important.

Carbon/metal oxide semiconductor materials based gas sensors have been extensively studied in recent years [11]. The carbonaceous materials such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have nanoscale example diameters and high specific surface areas, which can improve the effective adsorption of gases molecules [12]. These carbonaceous materials often have luminous properties and can be used as photocatalysts. They play as acceptor and donor of electrons under specific wavelength irradiation and are often used as a carrier library [13]. The size of CQD is usually less than 10 nm, and it has good luminous characteristics and electrical conductivity. A large number of photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be generated internally under photoexcitation [14]. CQDs can provide additional carrier charges after CQDs are formed into a composite material with a metal oxide semiconductor, and form a large number of active oxygen species for gas adsorption [15,16]. Compared with pure CQDs, N-doped CQDs have been reported to induce electron and hole separation effectively and reduce the work function [17,18]. This will be more conducive to the transfer of electrons to the semiconductor material, forming more gas adsorption active sites in the semiconductor oxide [19,20]. Optical fiber gas sensor has attracted wide attention because of its advantages of low cost and strong ability of shielding electromagnetic interference [21,22]. The evanescent field on the optical fiber surface is sensitive to the change of ambient refractive index, so the optical fiber gas sensor supports the detection of gas at room temperature [23–25]. The gas sensor is prepared by modifying the gas sensitive layer on the optical fiber surface. The refractive index change of the gas-sensitive layer caused by the change of gas concentration will affect the penetration depth of the evanescent field into the sensitive layer confining on the fiber surface, so the sensor will show high sensitivity to gas [26–28]. Optical fiber gas sensors can avoid high temperature dependence while achieving low power consumption compared with metal oxide semiconductor resistive gas sensors [29–31].

In this study, N-CQDs@In2O3 composite ordered porous films with a pore size of 500 nm were prepared by the sacrificial template method of PS microspheres. Optical fiber room temperature acetone gas sensor was prepared by deposition of thin film on hollow-core fiber (HCF) surface. As far as we know, this is the first application of ordered porous thin film structure in optical fiber gas sensor field. The experimental results verified a sensitivity of 7.1 pm/ppm for acetone gas under UV irradiation, which is ∼5.9 times higher than that without UV sensitization. The sensor has the advantages of room temperature operation, low limit of detection (LOD), high selectivity and moisture resistance. It has broad application prospects in acetone gas detection.

Citric acid, obtained in Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology. Co. Polystyrene microspheres (PS), purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Urea, purchased from Tianjin Regent Chemicals Co. Indium (Ⅲ) nitrate tetrahydrate (In(NO3)3·4 H2O), purchased from Shanghai Wokai Biotechnology Co. All reagents used in this work without further purification. Ultra-pure water was used in the whole experiment.

N-CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal method [32]. 3 g citric acid and 1 g urea were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water, and ultrasonic treatment was performed for 15 min. Then the mixed liquid was transferred to a stainless-steel reactor and treated at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, the yellow liquid was obtained and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 3 min to remove unreacted substances. Then the yellow liquid was dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 72 h to obtain N-CQDs solution. N-CQDs solution was dried at 60 ℃ for 48 h to obtain N-CQDs powder for characterization.

The fabrication process of optical fiber acetone gas sensor is shown in Fig. 1. The red dashed line box shows the preparation process of HMS. Single mode fiber (SMF) and HCF (inner diameter 19 μm, outer diameter 131 μm) are staggered and fused, the dislocation distance is 30 μm, the intercepted length of HCF is 1.5 cm, then the SMF is fused on the other side of HCF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CQDs@In2O3 ordered porous film
ce:section.ce:para: The PS microspheres with a diameter of 500 nm were dispersed in the mixed solution of deionized water and ethanol (volume ratio H2O: C2H6O=1: 1) by ultrasound, and then injected into the hydrophilic glass plate (volume ratio H2SO4: H2O2=1: 1 piranha solution soaked for 72 h) by syringe. At this time, the PS microspheres were spontaneously arranged into a regular two-dimensional plane structure due to the surface tension at the gas-liquid interface, and forming a layered film of glass plate-water film-PS microspheres. 20 mM In2(NO3)3 and 1 mg N-CQDs solution were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water, and the precursor solution was formed after full stirring. The glass plate-water film-PS microspheres layered film was placed in the precursor solution. The hydrophobicity of PS microspheres film made the glass plate-water film-PS microspheres layered film separate and formed PS microspheres film on the surface of the precursor solution. Stand for 10 min, waiting for the precursor solution to enter the pores between PS microspheres. The film is fished by the prepared optical fiber structure, and the film is attached to the sensing area of the optical fiber sensor by van der Waals force and surface tension. Then the optical fiber was transferred to a muffle furnace and calcined at 400 ℃ for 2 h. PS microspheres are volatilized at high temperature, and N-CQDs@In2O3 porous and ordered thin film structure is formed on the surface of optical fiber. In the same way, we fabricated the optical fiber sensor coated with pure In2O3 film to compare the gas sensing performance with N-CQDs@In2O3 porous and ordered film.

Density functional theory (DFT) is considered to provide the surface properties, adsorption behavior and reaction mechanism of materials [33–35]. The first principles calculations based on DFT are carried out to describe the changes of electronic energy levels and density of states during gas adsorption. CASTEP is used to optimize the model and DMol3 is used to calculate the In2O3(110) and N-CQDs@In2O3(110). Fig. 2(a) shows the optimized In2O3(110) crystal plane, and Fig. 2(b) shows the optimized N-CQDs@In2O3(110) plane. The exchange-correlation energy is calculated using Perdew, Burke and Emzerhof (PBE) of generalized gradient estimation (GGA). The In2O3(110) provides the better stability and reactivity performance for adsorbing the crystal surface in this study. The Energy cutoff is 400 eV and the k-point grid is 2x2x1. The adsorption energy can be calculated by Eq. (1):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas sensing system and experimental procedures
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the gas detection system of the optical fiber acetone gas sensor. The system consists of an amplified spontaneous emitter (ASE, C+L-band:1528–1603 nm), a well-sealed gas chamber (volume 2 L) with a size of 10×10×20 cm, made of acrylic sheet, a UV (λ=365 nm) LED light source (10 W, Shenzhen, Yingfeng Optoelectronics), gas bottles, an optical fiber acetone gas sensor and an optical spectrum analyser (OSA, resolution: 0.02 nm). The gas cylinder and the gas chamber are connected by an air duct with an inner diameter of 7.7 mm. The gas flow control adopts the Seven Star CS200 mass flowmeter (MFM) designed by Guangzhou Lexin Electronics Co., LTD. The range is 0–5 SLM, the response time is less than 0.8 s and it can reach ±1 % of the set point (ST). Acetone gas with concentration gradients of 50 ppm, 100 ppm, 150 ppm, 200 ppm, 250 ppm, 300 ppm and 350 ppm is introduced into the gas chamber in turn. Air should be passed to remove residual gas before injecting acetone gas. A dynamic detection platform (the black dotted box in Fig. 3) is established to study the dynamic characteristics of optical fiber gas sensors. The platform includes distributed feedback (DFB, Operating wavelength: 1550 nm, Line width: 1 nm) laser, photodetector (PD, Bandwidth: 10 MHz-3 dB) and oscilloscope (1 GHz).

The volatile gases mentioned in this study are injected into the gas chamber by different precision syringes. The reagents used in the experiment were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Including acetone (ACS, ≥ 99 %), ammonia (ACS, ≥ 99.5 %), aniline (ACS, ≥ 99.7 %), 2-butanone (ACS, ≥ 99.5 %), ethanol (ACS, ≥ 99.7 %), formaldehyde(ACS, ≥ 99 %). Acetone gas was drawn by syringe into a liquid acetone chemical reagent bottle [36]. The concentration of the gas to be measured is determined according to the antoine equation (Eq. 2) [37].
(2)
lg
P = A − B / ( T + C )
where P is the vapor pressure of the substance in millimeters of mercury (mmHg), T is the temperature. The volatile gases used in the experiments, A, B, and C, are constants are shown in Table 1. In the experiments, trace amounts of the gases were injected at a time through a syringe, so that the effect of flow rate could be neglected. The vapor pressure of the volatile gases used in this study is much greater at room temperature than that of water (2.33 kPa), so the effect of water vapor on the concentration of volatile gases can be ignored. Both CO2 and NO2 are supplied in standard cylinders (500 ppm concentration, 98 % purity). As shown in Fig. 3, air (20 % O2/ 80 % N2) is injected into the gas chamber via MFM1 for a period of time to remove interference from other polluting gases. In the case of a certain air flow, MFM2, which controls the CO2 flow rate, is adjusted to modulate the CO2 concentration in the gas chamber. The concentration can be determined by Eq. (3):

The humidity generation system is shown in the red dotted box in Fig. 3. Dry air with a fixed flow rate is injected into the air chamber through MFM1. Through MFM3, air with different flow rates after saturated NaCl solution is injected and air with different humidity can be obtained at different flow rates. Finally, dry air mixed with wet air can produce relative humidity in different concentration ranges.

The hollow malposition structure (HMS) guides the optical beam to propagate in the cladding of hollow core fiber (HCF), which can form evanescent field on the surface of HCF. This allows evanescent light to enter the gas-sensitive layer. The optical beam in SMF enters the cladding of HCF through misalignment fusion splicing, as illustrated in Fig. 4(a).

A part of the beam continues to propagate forward in HCF cladding. At the interface between the cladding and the air core, another part of light is refracted and propagated into the air core due to the difference of refractive index. The propagation light in the HCF cladding can excite the evanescent field on the fiber surface and interact with the N-CQDs@In2O3 gas sensing layer. When acetone gas molecules are adsorbed on N-CQDs@In2O3 thin films on HCF surface, the refractive index of N-CQDs@In2O3 thin films changes due to electron transfer and physical adsorption between gas molecules and sensitive materials [38]. The penetration depth of evanescent wave in the material can be calculated by Eq. (4):
(4)

Where, λ is the wavelength, n 1 is the refractive index of HCF cladding, n 2 is the refractive index of N-CQDs@In2O3 gas sensing layer, and θ is the incident angle of light. The change of refractive index of N-CQDs@In2O3 material causes the change of penetration depth of evanescent light in the gas sensing layer, which affects the propagation path and angle of transmitted light. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the optical path of light propagation in HCF cladding changes, which causes the change of light propagation in air core. The interference mode between them changes when the light returns to SMF. Different concentrations of acetone gas can cause different refractive index changes of N-CQDs@In2O3 thin films, resulting in different interference modes of light output. The optical interference mode is calculated by Eq. (5):

Where, L is the length of HCF.

The gas sensing mechanism of N-CQDs@In2O3 film is related to the change of resistance and refractive index when acetone gas molecules are adsorbed and desorbed by N-CQDs@In2O3 film [39–41]. As shown in Fig. 4(b) and Eqs. (6,7), carrier electrons of n-type semiconductor In2O3 are adsorbed by oxygen in air and become adsorbed oxygen O2 -, thus forming an electron depletion layer in In2O3 [42]. The resistance of N-CQDs@In2O3 composites increases with the decrease of carrier concentration. As shown in Eq. 4(c) and Eq. (8), when the sensor is placed in acetone gas environment, O2 - binds to acetone gas molecules. A large number of electrons are released into In2O3, accompanied by the generation of CO2 and H2O in this process [43]. The electron release process makes the electron depletion layer on the surface of In2O3 become thinner and the resistance of N-CQDs@In2O3 composites decreases [44]. The change of carrier density in N-CQDs@In2O3 composites results in the change of refractive index, which can modulate the evanescent field on the surface of optical fiber sensor.

N-CQDs can be used as excellent electron donors or acceptors and have excellent photoinduced electron transfer properties [45,46]. At the same time, they can form a heterojunction structure with semiconductor metal materials [47]. The oxygen adsorption kinetics of N-CQDs@In2O3 films irradiated by UV light is completely different from that of Fig. 4(b). As shown in Fig. 4(d), UV light induced N-CQDs produce efficient charge separation and photo-generated electrons-holes, and the ability of N-CQDs to inhibit electron-hole recombination can make photo-generated electrons transfer [48,49]. The work function of In2O3 (5.0 eV) [50] is significantly higher than that of N-CQDs (4.26 eV) [51]. Therefore, photo-generated electrons will move freely to In2O3 and form O2 - with more O2 on the surface (shown in Eq. (9)), which increases the number of reaction sites on the sensor surface. The bonding between O2(photo) - formed by these photogenerated electrons and the sensor is weak, which is more active than the traditional O2 - reaction [52,53]. This has made an outstanding contribution to the improvement of gas sensing performance. Photogenerated holes migrate to the sensor surface and combine with O2 - to form O2 (as shown in Eq. (10)), and recombine with electrons to form O2 -. When the sensor is exposed to acetone gas, besides the reaction in Eq. (7), the accelerated change shown in Eq. (11) occurs on the surface of N-CQDs@In2O3 film, which improves the gas sensing performance of the sensor [54].

Among them, ε
∞ is the dielectric constant and ρ is the principal value integral. There is an obvious functional relationship between the refractive index n and the conductivity σ of the material. At the same time, there is an inverse relationship between the resistance and the conductivity. Therefore, there is a dependence between the refractive index and the resistance of the material connected by the integral relationship.

As mentioned above, the mechanism by which gas adsorption and desorption affect the refractive index of materials is that when acetone gas molecules are adsorbed on the surface of N-CQDs@In2O3, the injection of electrons increases the conductivity of N-CQDs@In2O3. From Eq. (12), it can be seen that the imaginary part of the dielectric constant increases, and through equation Eq. (13), it further affects the real part of the dielectric constant. From Eq. (14), it can be seen that the refractive index of the material changes. When acetone gas molecules desorb on the surface of N-CQDs@In2O3, the resistance of the material increases, the conductivity decreases, the imaginary part of the dielectric constant changes, and finally affects the refractive index of the material. Therefore, the adsorption and desorption of acetone gas molecules lead to dynamic changes in the carrier density in the N-CQDs@In2O3 composite material, which in turn causes changes in conductivity and dielectric constant. These changes are ultimately reflected in the refractive index of the material, thereby affecting the propagation characteristics of light in the optical fiber by modulating the evanescent field on the surface of the optical fiber sensor. Through this mechanism, the optical fiber gas sensor can detect the concentration changes of acetone gas and achieve the purpose of gas detection.

DFT calculations show the specific details of the energy changes of the surface of N-CQDs@In2O3(110) after acetone adsorption. M1 illustrates the adsorption of acetone molecules on the surface of pure In2O3 ( Fig. 5(a)); in M2, acetone is adsorbed on the surface of N-CQDs of N-CQDs@In2O3(110) (Fig. 5(b)); and in M3, acetone is adsorbed on the surface of In2O3 of N-CQDs@In2O3(110) (Fig. 5(c)). The calculation results are shown in Table 2. It is seen that the adsorption energy of acetone adsorbed on N-CQDs@In2O3(110) is higher than that of pure In2O3 (-8.60993 eV), revealing that the N-CQDs significantly improves the adsorption capacity of acetone. The distance between acetone and N-CQDs in M2 decreases (2.764 Å), which may be due to the covalent bond between them [59,60], which leads to the adsorption of acetone gas molecules and the increase of adsorption energy. In addition, N-CQDs are rich in the π-electron structure, where the π-π stacking effect occurs between the π-electrons in acetone molecule and the π-electrons in N-CQDs [61–63], which leads to the overlap of electron clouds between them and increases the adsorption energy on the surface. M3 model has the largest adsorption energy (-38.9945 eV), which causes that the distance between ammonia and In2O3 is shortened to 2.177 Å. It concludes that In2O3 is the most stable adsorption point for acetone in N-CQDs@In2O3 [64].

Based on above calculation results, the density of states (DOS) of each atom in M3 has been obtained, as shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 6(a) shows that the 2p orbital of oxygen atom in In2O3 and the 2p orbital of carbon atom in N-CQDs are hybridized, thus changing the energy level of the interface and improving the reactivity near the interface, which will be beneficial to the adsorption of acetone gas molecules. Fig. 6(b)-(d) show that the 2p orbital of oxygen atom in acetone is hybrid with carbon atom, indium atom and nitrogen atom, which shows that chemical bonds can be formed among these elements, and the adsorption of acetone is mainly chemical adsorption [65].

The leading electron orbitals of N-CQDs@In2O3(110) and In2O3(110) crystal planes were studied ( Fig. 7 and Table 3). Compared with In2O3(110) crystal plane, the electron cloud of N-CQDs@In2O3(110) crystal plane is mainly distributed on the surface of N-CQDs and interface of N-CQDs and In2O3(110), where the more active electrons are beneficial to the adsorption of acetone molecules on the material surface. N-CQDs@In2O3(110) has a smaller energy level gap (LUMO-HOMO, 0.007469), which can effectively improve the transfer efficiency of electrons between the reaction surface and gas molecules, as well as the adsorption capacity of gas molecules [66–68]. In addition, the gap (0.1925) between LUMO of acetone and HOMO of N-CQDs@In2O3(110) becomes smaller, which means that the sensing reaction will be easier [69]. As shown in the Fig. 8, when acetone gas is adsorbed on the surface of N-CQDs@In2O3, the refractive index of the material in the infrared region increases. Therefore, the adsorption and desorption of acetone gas changes the refractive index of the material and affects the transmission of light in the optical fiber. Therefore, N-CQDs doped In2O3 is a promising candidate for acetone sensing.

Fig. 9(a) shows the scanning electron microscope (SEM) view of the optical fiber surface coated with PS microspheres film, and Fig. 9(b) shows a partial enlarged view. The surface layer of optical fiber is rough and uneven, which is caused by the uniform adsorption of PS microspheres on the surface of optical fiber and the viscosity of polymer. The microspheres were filled with N-CQDs/In2(NO3)3 precursor solution. When PS microspheres were removed at high temperature, the fiber surface became smooth and N-CQDs/In2O3 film remained on the fiber surface. Fig. 9(c) is a SEM view of the fiber surface after high temperature calcination, and Fig. 9(d) is a partial enlarged view. N-CQDs@In2O3 thin film was formed on the surface of fiber by the precursor solution of N-CQDs/In2(NO3)3. PS microspheres decomposed at high temperature, forming orderly porous structure on the surface of the thin film. Therefore, N-CQDs@In2O3 ordered porous films were successfully synthesized on the fiber surface.

The N-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 10(a), the TEM diagram of N-CQDs shows that the prepared N-CQDs have a particle size of about 6 nm and have good dispersion. The size of N-CQDs prepared in this study is less than 10 nm, which is basically consistent with previous studies in terms of shape and size [70]. This laid a foundation for the preparation of N-CQDs@In2O3 composites. Fig. 10(b) shows the emission spectrum of N-CQDs under UV light excitation. It can be seen from the figure that the excitation wavelength is 446 nm and the N-CQDs solution emits fluorescence. When irradiated by UV light, N-CQDs can emit strong fluorescence, which indicates the successful preparation of CQDs. Fig. 10(c) is the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of N-CQDs, which mainly shows the peaks of O, N and C, indicating the successful incorporation of N into CQDs.

Fig. 10(d) shows the SEM of N-CQDs@In2O3 thin film. The pores on the thin film are uniformly and orderly distributed, and the diameter of the pores is about 500 nm, which matches the diameter of PS microspheres. This porous and ordered structure is conducive to the full diffusion of gas on the surface of gas sensitive film and full contact with gas sensitive materials, which increases the contact area between gas and materials [71]. Fig. 10(e) shows the element mapping image of N-CQDs@In2O3, and Fig. 10(f-h) shows the mapping image of In, O and C elements, respectively. N-CQDs was successfully doped into In2O3 and various elements were uniformly distributed in N-CQDs@In2O3. This uniform distribution is beneficial to improve the synergistic effect between N-CQDs and In2O3, and improve the gas sensing performance. Fig. 10(i) is the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrum of N-CQDs@In2O3, showing the percentages of various elements. The percentages of In, O and C are 32.3 %, 46.7 % and 21.1 %, respectively. It is worth noting that the doping content of N element is too low to be shown in EDS spectrum. At the same time, the EDS elemental analysis results showed that the content of carbon was about 21.1 %, which further indicated the incorporation of N-CQDs in the N-CQDs@In2O3 porous film.

The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 11(a) show that In2O3 has strong absorption for light less than 450 nm and has strong UV light absorption capacity, which is consistent with previous reports [72]. In addition, N-CQDs@In2O3 has a greater absorption value for light with a wavelength greater than 350 nm. This may be because N-CQDs itself has good UV light absorption capacity [73]. The addition of N-CQDs improves the absorption capacity of In2O3 for UV light and helps to generate more photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the gas adsorption capacity of gas-sensitive materials [74,75]. Fig. 11(b) shows the (αhν)2-(hν) curve obtained using the Tauc plotting equation. From the curve, it can be seen that the estimated band gap values of In2O3 and N-CQDs@In2O3 are 3.02 eV and 2.89 eV, respectively. The reduction in the band gap proves that effective electron transfer is formed between N-CQDs and In2O3 [76], and the electron transition ability is improved, which helps to improve the gas sensing performance of gas-sensitive materials. At the same time, this is also one of the important reasons for the enhanced light absorption ability of N-CQDs@In2O3 [77].

Fig. 12(a) shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) of pure In2O3 and N-CQDs@In2O3, where the typical diffraction peaks at 21.5°, 30.6°, 35.5°, 51.1°, and 60.7° correspond to the (2 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (4 4 0) and (6 2 2) crystal planes of cubic In2O3, respectively (PDF#06–0416). Due to the low content of N-CQDs, the XRD of N-CQDs@In2O3 does not show obvious characteristic peaks of N-CQDs. However, in the XRD of N-CQDs@ In2O3, the peak of the crystal plane (2 1 1) is reduced compared to the baseline, which may be because the introduction of N-CQDs causes local defects in the crystal structure and affects the diffraction intensity of the crystal plane [78].

The fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of N-CQDs@In2O3 is shown in Fig. 12(b), which shows a variety of characteristic peaks: 3430.2 cm⁻¹ (-OH), 1625.2 cm⁻¹ (CO), 1577.9 cm⁻¹ (C-C), 1443.5 cm⁻¹ (C-N), 1109.4 cm⁻¹ (C-O) and 464.3 cm⁻¹ (In-O). Among them, the In-O stretching vibration peak of 464.3 cm⁻¹ is related to the In2O3 in the composite material. The C-C stretching vibration peak of 1577.9 cm⁻¹ is related to the aromatic structure of N-CQDs [79], and the C-N stretching vibration peak of 1443.5 cm⁻¹ indicates the successful doping of nitrogen elements [80]. In addition, the OH at 3430.2 cm⁻¹, CO at 1625.2 cm⁻¹, and C-O stretching vibration peaks at 1109.4 cm⁻¹ show the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs [81]. The existence of these chemical bonds not only confirms the existence of N-CQDs and the characteristics of their surface functional groups, but also reveals their important role in the composite material.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis results show the detailed elemental composition of N-CQDs@In2O3. Fig. 12(c) shows the characteristic peaks related to In in N-CQDs@In2O3, indicating the successful preparation of In2O3. The characteristic peaks of O1s at 530.6 eV and 532.5 eV in Fig. 12(d) are related to CO bonds and C-O-C bonds, respectively [82], which is mainly because N-CQDs are functionalized with oxygen-containing functional groups during the preparation process. The characteristic peak at 400.1 eV in Fig. 12(e) is related to C-N bonds, which indicates the successful incorporation of nitrogen elements in N-CQDs and the formation of chemical bonds with carbon elements. Fig. 12(f) shows that C1s is decomposed into 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.4 eV, which correspond to C-C, C-O and CO bonds, respectively. Among them, the sp2 C (C-C) elements related to N-CQDs account for up to 89.2 %, which is attributed to the incorporation of N-CQDs in N-CQDs@In2O3 [83]. The XPS analysis results show the elemental distribution of N-CQDs in the composite material, further confirming the existence of N-CQDs.

TEM can show the shape and size of N-CQDs. Fig. 13(a) and (b) are TEM of N-CQDs in N-CQDs@In2O3. It can be seen from the figure that N-CQDs are dispersed on the surface of In2O3, with a diameter of about 6 nm, which is consistent with the size of pure N-CQDs prepared. In addition, the lattice spacing of 0.33 nm in the figure is related to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of graphite [84], so the existence of N-CQDs can be confirmed by TEM.

Fig. 14(a) shows the response curve of the optical fiber sensor coated with pure In2O3 film to acetone gas with concentration of 0–350 ppm. The spectrum shows obvious red shift when the acetone gas in the environment gradually increases. In2O3 as an n-type semiconductor can make O2 in air adsorb its carrier electrons and become an adsorbed oxygen species O2 -. When acetone gas exists in the environment, acetone gas will react with O2 - as shown in Eq. (5). This leads to the change of carrier density on the surface of In2O3 thin films and changes the refractive index of the films. Finally, the penetration depth of evanescent wave on the fiber surface changes and the spectrum red shifts. Fig. 14(b) shows the response curve of the sensor under UV light irradiation. Fig. 14(c) shows the sensitivity comparison curve of the optical fiber sensor based on In2O3 thin film without and with UV light irradiation. The gas sensitivity of the sensor is 0.4 pm/ppm without UV light irradiation, but 0.9 pm/ppm under UV light irradiation, which is about ∼2.25 times difference for these two values. The increase of sensitivity may be due to the excitation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs in In2O3 materials by UV light. Some of the photo-generated electrons combine with O2 to become O2 - and participate in the gas reaction of Eq. (5). However, there is a certain possibility of recombination between photo-generated electrons and holes [85], so the gas sensing performance of the sensor has not been significantly improved.

Fig. 14(d) shows the response curve of an optical fiber gas sensor coated with N-CQDs@In2O3 film to acetone gas. Its gas sensitivity is 1.2 pm/ppm (green curve in Fig. 10(f)), which shows better gas sensitivity than pure In2O3 material. This benefits from the fact that the smaller particle size of N-CQDs increases the specific surface area of materials and provides more active sites for gas reactionp [86,87]. Fig. 14(e) shows the spectral response curve of optical fiber gas sensor based on N-CQDs@In2O3 thin film under UV light irradiation. More significants redshift was observed in the spectrum. The sensitivity is improved to 7.1 pm/ppm (red curve in Fig. 14(f)). The reason for the increased sensitivity of the sensor is that a large number of photo-generated electron-hole pairs are formed in N-CQDs by UV light, and the difference between the work functions of N-CQDs and In2O3 makes the photo-generated electrons flow to In2O3 and form more reactive oxygen species O2 - with O2 on its surface, which greatly increases the concentration of O2 - on the surface of N-CQDs@In2O3 composites. This provides more sites for the adsorption of acetone gas molecules. Therefore, the sensor shows higher gas sensitivity, which is increased by ∼7.9 times compared with the optical fiber sensor coated with UV light sensitized pure In2O3 film. The sensitivity is increased by ∼5.9 times compared with the optical fiber sensor coated with N-CQDs@In2O3 film without UV light sensitization.

Dynamic performance is one of the important indexes to evaluate the performance of gas sensing. The response sensitivity, response time and recovery time are studied. Fig. 15(a) shows the dynamic response curve of the optical fiber sensor coated with N-CQDs@In2O3 film with and without UV light sensitization. When the acetone gas concentration changes in gradient form, the output of the sensor shows good dynamic response capability. Fig. 15(a) also shows that the LOD of the sensor is 500 ppb, which is of great significance for the detection of trace gases in practice. Fig. 15(b) shows the sensitivity curve of the sensor to acetone gas under dynamic detection. The sensitivity of the sensor without UV light sensitization is 0.05 mv/ppm, and the sensitivity with UV light sensitization is 0.3 mv/ppm, which is increased by 6 times. The results are consistent with those of static detection. Fig. 15(c) is the time response curve of the sensor without UV light sensitization. The sensor has a response time of 7.5 s and a recovery time of 9.4 s. Because the ordered porous membrane structure provides more channels for gas flow and accelerates the adsorption and desorption of gas [88]. Fig. 15(d) shows the time response curve of the sensor being sensitized by UV light. The response time is 6.2 s and the recovery time is 7.1 s. The better time response characteristics may be due to the increase of reactive sites on the surface of the film by UV light.

Selectivity is one of the important properties of gas sensor. Fig. 16(a) shows the response curve of optical fiber acetone sensor coated with N-CQDs@In2O3 film to ammonia gas, aniline, formaldehyde, ethanol, 2-butanone, NO2, CO and acetone gas. Acetone gas sensor selectivity above 7.4, better than typical confounding factors. Therefore, the sensor has good selectivity for acetone gas. The 40 ppm acetone gas was detected several times to verify the stability of the optical fiber sensor. Fig. 16(b) is the response curve of the sensor at 40 ppm, which is almost unchanged and stable at 0.043 V and shows that the sensor has good stability. The influence of humidity on acetone gas sensing performance was studied by changing the relative humidity of the environment. Fig. 16(c) and (d) show the humidity response curve and humidity sensitivity curve, respectively. The humidity sensitivity is 0.02 mv/% RH. The influence on the gas detection error is ±0.07 ppm when the humidity changes by 1 %RH, which can be basically ignored. The sensor was placed in a gas chamber with an acetone concentration of 300 ppm for 14 days, and Fig. 16(e) shows the spectral changes of the sensor during this cycle. The results show that the spectra of the sensor did not drift significantly, thus the sensor has good stability, which is important in the practical use of the sensor.

Table 4 compares the optical fiber acetone sensor proposed in this work with those previously reported. Compared with the plastic optical fibers (POFs) acetone gas sensor, this sensor has a lower LOD [89]. The optical fiber acetone sensor based on ZnO-TiO2 needs an operating temperature of 87 ℃ [90]. SnO2/MoS2-coated evanescent wave fiber optic sensor can work at room temperature but requires longer response time [91,92]. Gas sensors based on semiconductor heterojunction need higher working temperature [93,94]. Therefore, the optical fiber gas sensor proposed in this study has the advantages of LOD, fast response time and room temperature detection. Compared with the above-mentioned gas sensors, it may be more suitable for practical applications.

In this study, the gas sensing characteristics of room temperature acetone sensor based on N-CQDs@In2O3 ordered porous film are studied. The mechanism of UV sensitization of the sensor is explored. N-CQDs@In2O3 ordered porous film was prepared on the surface of optical fiber by template method, and acetone gas detection was realized. The experimental results show that UV radiation effectively improves the sensitivity to 7.1 pm/ppm, which is ∼5.9 times higher compared to the non-UV case. In addition, the sensor has a LOD of 500 ppb, a response time of 6.2 s, a recovery time of 7.1 s. It also performs the high selectivity to acetone gas, good moisture resistance, and the error caused by experimental humidity is demonstrated to be only 0.07 ppm/%RH. The probe has the advantages of low cost, good preparation repeatability and low concentration acetone gas detection, which is of great significance for the detection of diabetes mellitus.
Yannan Wang: Writing – original draft. Jin Li: Writing – review & editing. Fanli Meng: Writing – review & editing. Liyun Qin: Conceptualization. Zhenyu Yuan: Data curation.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yannan Wang was born in Zhangjiakou, Hebei Province, China, in 1998. He received the Master’s degree in Detection Technology and Automation Device in July, 2022. He is currently purchasing a Doctor’s Degree in Detection Technology and Automatic Devices supervised by A/Prof. Jin Li in the College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, China. His research interests are microfiber sensor, optical integration, Optoelectronics detection and automatic control methods.

Jin Li was born in Yuncheng, Shanxi Province, China, in 1983. He has received the Bachelor’s degree in Electronics Science and Technology in 2007, the Master’s degree and Doctor’s degree in Physical Electronics respectively in 2009 and 2014, at Harbin Institute of Technology (HIT), China.He servers the Research Fellow and Ph.D. supervisor in the College of Mechanical and Electronic Engineering, Northwest A&F University, China. His research interests include micro/nano-structured photonics, nanomaterials’ nonlinear optics, micro/nanofiber sensors and integrated optical devices.Dr. Li has charged for more than 10 national and province projects and published more than 50 SCI-indexed papers in the role of first or corresponding author.

Fanli Meng was born in Tieling, Liaoning Province China, in 1980. He received his Bachelor’s degree from Nanjing University of Science and Technology in 2002; In 2005, he obtained a Master's degree in Hefei Institute of Intelligent Machinery, Chinese Academy of Sciences; In 2009, he obtained a doctor's degree at the University of Science and Technology of China.He worked as a research Fellow in Hefei Institute of Intelligent Machinery, Chinese Academy of Sciences from 2005 to 2017. Since December 2016, he has been a professor in the College of Information Science and Engineering at Northeastern University, China.Prof. Meng has been in charge of 4 projects supported by National Natural Science foundation of China. He has published more than 90 papers in the SCI indexed peer review journals, such as Sensors and Actuators B, IEEE Transactions on Nanotechnology, TrAC-Trends in Analytical Chemistry, Analytica Chimica Acta and IEEE Sensors Journal. Up to now, all these papers have been cited by others for more than 3000 times and the h-index is 32. Among them, three were selected to be ESI highly cited papers. The most highly cited paper was cited over 400 times by others. He has got 8 authorized patents. He also achieved many awards such as the CAIA Youth Award from China Association for Instrumental Analysis, the first grade of prize in Anhui Natural Science Award, the Anhui Outstanding Academic Paper Award in Natural Science, etc. He is an associated editor of the journal of Micro & Nano Letters and is also a member of the editorial board of the Journal of Sensors, Acta Automatica Sinica, etc. Main research areas include: gas sensing nanomaterials and devices, chemical sensors and trace detection technology.
